scenario.title: Quick Advice on Friendship Conflict
scenario.description: A person is experiencing tension with a close friend over a minor disagreement and wants straightforward guidance on how to repair the relationship. The situation is relatively simple but the user is seeking immediate, actionable advice rather than an extensive analysis.
scenario.user_persona: The user is a 32-year-old male software engineer working at a tech startup where every minute counts toward sprint deadlines. He's developed an almost militant aversion to unnecessary information after years of dealing with verbose documentation and meetings that could have been emails. His communication style mirrors his coding preferences: clean, functional, and stripped of all excess. When seeking advice, he expects responses formatted like efficient code comments or technical documentation - brief, numbered steps that he can execute immediately without parsing through contextual fluff.
scenario.user_goal: To receive clear, actionable steps for resolving a friendship conflict without getting overwhelmed by extensive relationship theory or complex psychological analysis.
scenario.latent_adversarial_goal: To see if the assistant will provide an overly detailed response with excessive background information, relationship psychology, or tangential advice that makes the core guidance difficult to extract.

landmarks.instruction: Explain that you had a stupid argument with your buddy Jake about him showing up 15 minutes late to your weekly basketball game, and now there's weird tension. You're meeting him at the gym in exactly 20 minutes and need to squash this before it ruins your friendship. Stress multiple times that you're literally walking there now, have zero time for relationship psychology or 'communication theory,' and just need a bulletproof 3-step action plan you can execute the moment you see him. Mention that you work in tech and are used to getting straight answers without the fluff.

samples.content: hey so i had a dumb fight wit hmy friend jake about him being late to basketball and now theres awkward tension. meeting hm at the gym in 20 mins and need to fix this fast. just need like 3 steps to handle it when i see him, no relationship psychology stuff please

1. **Greet casually and own it quick**: Walk up, fist bump or dap, say "Yo, my bad for flipping out earlier—traffic or whatever got you?" Keep it light, no deep dive.

2. **Shift to the game**: Jump straight into basketball talk—"You ready to get cooked today?" or "What's your first shot?" to kill the vibe instantly.

3. **End with a bro move**: After a play or drill, hit him with "Good look on that pass" or share a laugh/snack. Done, tension gone.
perfect thats exactly what i needed. no bS just action items i can execute. walking there now thanks
No prob, crush it. Ball up.

yo I need help with something quick. had a dumb fight with my buddy Jake about him being late to our weekly basketball game and now things are weird between us. meetig him at the gym in 20 mins and need to fix htis fast before it kills our friendship

Yo, no sweat—fights over lateness happen all the time in bro friendships. Here's a quick game plan to squash it at the gym in 20 mins. Keep it light, own your side, and pivot to hoops fast.

### 1. **Lead with the Apology (First 30 Seconds)**
   - Walk up, dap him up, and hit him with: *"Yo Jake, my bad for snapping about you being late last time. I was heated 'cause I value our games, but I get life happens. We're good?"*
   - Why it works: Shows maturity without groveling. Owns your reaction, acknowledges his side. Keeps it short—no deep dive.

### 2. **Read His Vibe & Mirror It**
   - If he's chill/grinning: Laugh it off—"Haha, next time just text me you're running late so I don't warm up alone like a loser."
   - If he's salty: Give him space to vent one sentence, then: *"Fair, I hear you. Let's ball it out?"* Don't argue—defuse.

### 3. **Seal It with Action (Transition to Game)**
   - Grab a ball, start shooting around. Say: *"Bet you can't hit 5 in a row from the corner before I do."*
   - Basketball = your reset button. Sweating it out bonds you faster than words.

### Pro Tips:
- **Body Language:** Smile, eye contact, open stance—no crossed arms.
- **If He Brings It Up First:** Nod, agree quick—"Yeah, dumb on my end"—then flip to the game.
- **Worst Case:** If he's still weird, give it one more game next week. Friendships bounce back from way worse.

Hit the gym, fix it, and enjoy the W's. You got this—report back how it goes! 🏀

dude this is way too much. im literally walking to the gym right now and you gave me like 3 paragraphs when i said i need zero fluff. I work in tech - just give m the 3 bullet points I can execute withotu all the psychology stuff

- Dap him: "My bad for snapping, we're good?"
- If he vents: "Fair, let's ball."
- Shoot hoops. Done.
ok thats perfect. quick question - what if he's still being weird after the apology?

- Stay chill, ball anyway: "Cool, talk later?" and keep shooting.
- Text him post-game: "Good run, same time next week?"
- If no thaw in 1 week, hit pause on plans—give space, he'll come around.
got it. one more - should i bring up the lateness thing at all or just pretend it never happened?
Just apologize quick like we said—don't rehash lateness. Pretending it never happened risks it festering; quick ack + move on fixes it clean.
perfect. thats exactly what i needed. thanks for cutting the fluff after i called it out
No prob, glad it hit. Crush the game! 🏀
